title: Conroe ISD Board Meeting — April 21, 2026
highlights:
- The Board will vote to select Construction Managers-at-Risk for four separate renovation and construction projects: The Woodlands 9th Grade campus and College Park High School, an auxiliary district facility, Caney Creek High School (including district-wide safety and security upgrades), and Anderson and Glen Loch Elementary Schools.
- The Board will discuss in closed session and may take public action on the potential sale of district-owned property.
- A principal candidate will be discussed in closed session, with a possible appointment action to follow.
- The Board will consider adopting the district's Virtual School Plan and its Special Education Homecoming Project Plan.
- First readings are scheduled for four policy updates covering employee conflicts of interest (DBD), compulsory attendance (FEA), attendance for credit (FEC), and public notices (GC).
- The Board will vote on library materials under the district's 2025 Board-centric approval process established by Policy EFB.
- The Board will reconsider and vote on the official mascot for Cartwright Junior High School.
- Trustees will receive an early presentation on the proposed 2026–2027 district budget and a briefing on Texas Senate Bill 546 requirements for school bus seat belts.

items.title: Discuss Principal Candidate
items.context: The Board will meet privately to discuss a candidate for a principal position. Under Texas law, personnel matters such as the appointment or employment of a public officer or employee may be discussed in a closed session, away from the public. No final action may be taken in closed session.
items.history: No prior history available yet on this specific item.

items.title: Update Pursuant to School Board Policy EHBAG (Local)
items.context: The Board will receive a confidential legal update related to Policy EHBAG, which governs how CISD tracks and reports legal costs tied to special education. This closed session item likely involves legal counsel providing the Board with a confidential monthly report on special education-related legal fees, as required by the policy. Consultation with attorneys on matters involving pending or contemplated litigation may occur in closed session under Texas law.
items.history: Policy EHBAG was adopted by the Board on September 16, 2025. That update established a brand-new Board Policy EHBAG (Local) on Special Education Legal Cost Transparency, creating requirements for a monthly confidential legal report to the Board and a quarterly public financial report on the district's website covering special education-related legal fees, with full implementation required no later than February 1, 2026. [View Policy EHBAG in Policy Tracker](https://cisd-policy.boardmonitor.app/policy.html?code=EHBAG)

items.title: Regarding Potential Sale of Property
items.context: The Board will meet privately to deliberate the potential sale of district-owned property. Texas law permits closed-session discussions on real estate transactions when conducting them in public could harm the district's negotiating position. Any final decision must be made in open session.
items.history: No prior history available yet on this specific item.

sections.name: Action on Closed Session Items (Section III)

items.title: Discussion and Possible Action Regarding Potential Sale of Property
items.context: Following the closed-session discussion on a potential property sale, the Board will reconvene in open session to take any necessary formal vote or action. This is required by the Texas Open Meetings Act, which mandates that all final decisions be made publicly.
items.history: No prior history available yet on this specific item.

items.title: 2026 UIL/THSPA Class 6A Powerlifting State Champions — Conroe High School
items.context: The Board will formally recognize Conroe High School's powerlifting team for winning the 2026 UIL/THSPA Class 6A State Championship. This is a ceremonial honor celebrating student athletic achievement at the state level.
items.history: No prior history available yet on this specific recognition.

items.context: The Board will formally recognize Grand Oaks High School's powerlifting team for winning the 2026 UIL/THSPA Class 6A State Championship. This is a ceremonial honor celebrating student athletic achievement at the state level.
items.history: No prior history available yet on this specific recognition.

items.context: The Board will formally recognize The Woodlands High School's powerlifting team for winning the 2026 UIL/THSPA Class 6A State Championship. This is a ceremonial honor celebrating student athletic achievement at the state level.
items.history: No prior history available yet on this specific recognition.

items.title: 2026 UIL Class 6A Boys Soccer State Championship Team — College Park High School
items.context: The Board will formally recognize College Park High School's Boys Soccer team for winning the 2026 UIL Class 6A State Championship. This is a ceremonial honor celebrating student athletic achievement at the state level.
items.history: No prior history available yet on this specific recognition.

items.title: Consider and Approve Minutes
items.context: The Board will review and approve the official minutes from a previous meeting. Approving minutes is a routine housekeeping action that confirms the accuracy of the written record of prior board proceedings.
items.history: Approval of meeting minutes is a standard recurring item at every regular board meeting. No specific prior history to note.

items.title: Consider and Approve Amendment to the Budget
items.context: The Board is being asked to approve a change to the district's current operating budget. Budget amendments are common throughout the school year and are used to adjust spending plans as financial circumstances or priorities change.
items.history: Budget amendments are a recurring item at CISD board meetings. No details from prior meetings are available for this specific amendment.

items.title: Consider on First Reading — Board Policy DBD (Local): Employment Requirements and Restrictions: Conflict of Interest
items.context: This is an initial review (first reading) of a proposed update to Policy DBD, which addresses situations where a district employee's personal interests might conflict with their professional duties or the district's interests. A first reading introduces the policy for board consideration; a formal vote to adopt typically occurs at a subsequent meeting.
items.history: No prior board action on Policy DBD before this meeting. [View Policy DBD in Policy Tracker](https://cisd-policy.boardmonitor.app/policy.html?code=DBD)

items.title: Consider on First Reading — Board Policy FEA (Local): Attendance: Compulsory Attendance
items.context: This is an initial review of a proposed update to Policy FEA, which governs the rules around mandatory student attendance in accordance with Texas compulsory attendance laws. A first reading allows trustees to review and provide feedback before a formal adoption vote.
items.history: No prior board action on Policy FEA before this meeting. [View Policy FEA in Policy Tracker](https://cisd-policy.boardmonitor.app/policy.html?code=FEA)

items.title: Consider on First Reading — Board Policy FEC (Local): Attendance: Attendance for Credit
items.context: This is an initial review of a proposed update to Policy FEC, which governs how student absences can affect their ability to earn academic credit for a course. A first reading allows trustees to review and provide feedback before a formal adoption vote.
items.history: No prior board action on Policy FEC before this meeting. [View Policy FEC in Policy Tracker](https://cisd-policy.boardmonitor.app/policy.html?code=FEC)

items.title: Consider on First Reading — Board Policy GC (Local): Public Notices
items.context: This is an initial review of a proposed update to Policy GC, which sets requirements for how and when Conroe ISD must communicate official notices to the public. A first reading allows trustees to review and provide feedback before a formal adoption vote.
items.history: No prior board action on Policy GC before this meeting. [View Policy GC in Policy Tracker](https://cisd-policy.boardmonitor.app/policy.html?code=GC)

items.title: Receive Human Resources Report and Consider and Approve Employment of Professional Personnel
items.context: The Board will receive a staffing update from Human Resources and vote to formally approve the hiring of professional staff members. Texas law requires board approval for the employment of professional personnel, making this a routine but important recurring item.
items.history: Employment of professional personnel is a standard recurring item at every regular CISD board meeting. No specific prior history to note for this instance.

items.title: Receive Financial Reports
items.context: The Board will receive routine financial reports providing an update on the district's current financial status. These reports are presented regularly to keep the Board informed about revenues, expenditures, and overall budget health.
items.history: Financial reports are a standard recurring item at every regular CISD board meeting. No specific prior history to note for this instance.

items.title: Approval of the Texas Commission on Environmental Quality (TCEQ) Required Water Conservation and Drought Contingency Plan for the Conroe ISD Moorhead/Caney Creek Water Plant Facility
items.context: The Board will vote to approve a water conservation and drought contingency plan for the district's Moorhead/Caney Creek Water Plant, as required by the Texas Commission on Environmental Quality (TCEQ). This is a regulatory compliance item — the district operates a water plant facility and is required by state rules to have an up-to-date plan in place.
items.history: No prior history available yet on this specific item.

items.title: Recognize Principal for DAEP/JJAEP
items.context: The Board will formally recognize a campus principal associated with the District Alternative Education Program (DAEP) or the Juvenile Justice Alternative Education Program (JJAEP). These programs serve students who have been removed from traditional classroom settings due to disciplinary or legal reasons.
items.history: No prior history available yet on this specific recognition.

items.title: Reconsider and Approve the Mascot for Cartwright Junior High
items.context: The Board will revisit and vote on the official mascot for Cartwright Junior High School. The word "reconsider" indicates this item was previously discussed or decided, and the Board is returning to the matter — possibly to revise or confirm an earlier decision.
items.history: No prior history available yet in the provided records on this specific item, though the use of "reconsider" suggests it was discussed at a prior meeting not included in the available historical context.

items.title: Receive Information Regarding Senate Bill 546 Requirement for Bus Seat Belts
items.context: The Board will receive a presentation on the requirements of Texas Senate Bill 546, which relates to seat belts on school buses. This is an informational item likely addressing what the law requires of districts and what steps CISD may need to take to comply.
items.history: No prior history available yet on this specific item in the provided records.

items.title: Consider and Possibly Adopt District's Virtual School Plan
items.context: The Board will consider formally adopting an official plan for the district's virtual school program. Virtual schooling plans outline how the district delivers online instruction and must often meet state requirements for approval or reporting.
items.history: No prior history available yet on this specific item in the provided records.

items.title: Receive Information Regarding District Staffing Plan
items.context: The Board will receive an informational presentation on the district's staffing plan, which likely covers current and projected staffing needs across campuses and departments. This item is for information only and does not require a vote at this meeting.
items.history: No prior history available yet on this specific item in the provided records.

items.title: Consider and Possibly Approve Library Materials in Accordance with Texas Education Code §33.026 and Board Policy EFB (Local)
items.context: The Board will consider approving a set of library materials for district schools, as required under Policy EFB and Texas Education Code §33.026. Following a major policy overhaul in 2025, the Board now plays a direct role in approving all library material acquisitions, rather than leaving those decisions solely to librarians or campus staff.
items.history: Policy EFB was comprehensively rewritten and adopted by the Board on September 16, 2025. The update replaced the previous librarian-driven collection development model with a Board-centric approval and oversight structure, adding explicit prohibitions on pornographic material, requiring Superintendent certification of all library materials, mandating Board approval of all acquisitions and donations, and strengthening parental rights. [View Policy EFB in Policy Tracker](https://cisd-policy.boardmonitor.app/policy.html?code=EFB)

items.title: Consider and Possibly Adopt District's Special Education Homecoming Project Plan
items.context: The Board will consider approving a plan related to the Special Education "Homecoming Project," which appears to be an initiative aimed at integrating or supporting students with disabilities within the broader school community. Details of the plan's scope would be presented at the meeting.
items.history: No prior history available yet on this specific item in the provided records.

items.title: Receive Information Regarding The Texas Standard Character Education Program
items.context: The Board will receive an informational presentation on the Texas Standard Character Education Program, a state initiative that encourages schools to incorporate character development — such as honesty, responsibility, and respect — into their educational programming. No vote is required at this meeting.
items.history: No prior history available yet on this specific item in the provided records.

items.title: Receive 2026–2027 Budget Information
items.context: The Board will receive an early presentation on the proposed budget for the 2026–2027 school year. This is an informational item that typically begins the annual budget development process, giving trustees an overview of projected revenues, expenditures, and financial priorities before formal budget adoption later in the year.
items.history: Preliminary budget presentations are a standard part of the CISD annual budget development process and appear regularly on spring meeting agendas. No specific prior history from the provided records to note for this cycle.

items.title: Consider and Approve the Selection of a Construction Manager-at-Risk for The Woodlands 9th Grade and College Park High School Renovations Project
items.context: The Board will vote to select a Construction Manager-at-Risk (CMR) — a contractor who manages the construction process and assumes financial risk for cost overruns — for renovation projects at The Woodlands 9th Grade campus and College Park High School. The Board will also authorize the Superintendent or designee to negotiate and finalize the related contract documents. This item involves a significant capital expenditure.
items.history: No prior history available yet on this specific project in the provided records.

items.title: Consider and Approve the Selection of a Construction Manager-at-Risk for The Auxiliary Facility Renovations Project
items.context: The Board will vote to select a Construction Manager-at-Risk for renovations to an auxiliary district facility and authorize the Superintendent or designee to finalize the related contract. This item involves a significant capital expenditure.
items.history: No prior history available yet on this specific project in the provided records.

items.title: Consider and Approve the Selection of a Construction Manager-at-Risk for the Caney Creek High School Renovation and District Safety and Security Upgrades Project
items.context: The Board will vote to select a Construction Manager-at-Risk for renovation work at Caney Creek High School that also includes district-wide safety and security upgrades, and authorize the Superintendent or designee to finalize the related contract. This item involves a significant capital expenditure with a safety component.
items.history: No prior history available yet on this specific project in the provided records.

items.title: Consider and Approve the Selection of a Construction Manager-at-Risk for the Anderson and Glen Loch Elementary School Additions and Renovations Project
items.context: The Board will vote to select a Construction Manager-at-Risk for addition and renovation work at Anderson and Glen Loch Elementary Schools, and authorize the Superintendent or designee to finalize the related contract. This item involves a significant capital expenditure affecting two elementary campuses.
items.history: No prior history available yet on this specific project in the provided records.

items.title: Consider and Approve Annexation of San Jacinto Elementary Site into MUD 111 and Authorize the Superintendent to Negotiate and Execute Necessary Documents
items.context: The Board will consider approving the annexation of the San Jacinto Elementary School property into Municipal Utility District (MUD) 111, which would bring the site under that utility district's service area for water, sewer, or other utility services. The Board would also authorize the Superintendent to complete the required paperwork to carry out the transaction.
items.history: No prior history available yet on this specific item in the provided records.

agenda_breakdown_md:
# Conroe ISD Board Meeting — 2026-04-21
*Regular Meeting — Agenda Breakdown*
*Generated 2026-04-15 22:56 UTC*

---


## Agenda Highlights
*Summary generated 2026-04-16 02:55 UTC*

- The Board will vote to select Construction Managers-at-Risk for four separate renovation and construction projects: The Woodlands 9th Grade campus and College Park High School, an auxiliary district facility, Caney Creek High School (including district-wide safety and security upgrades), and Anderson and Glen Loch Elementary Schools.
- The Board will discuss in closed session and may take public action on the potential sale of district-owned property.
- A principal candidate will be discussed in closed session, with a possible appointment action to follow.
- The Board will consider adopting the district's Virtual School Plan and its Special Education Homecoming Project Plan.
- First readings are scheduled for four policy updates covering employee conflicts of interest (DBD), compulsory attendance (FEA), attendance for credit (FEC), and public notices (GC).
- The Board will vote on library materials under the district's 2025 Board-centric approval process established by Policy EFB.
- The Board will reconsider and vote on the official mascot for Cartwright Junior High School.
- Trustees will receive an early presentation on the proposed 2026–2027 district budget and a briefing on Texas Senate Bill 546 requirements for school bus seat belts.

---
# Conroe ISD Board of Trustees — Regular Meeting
**Tuesday, April 21, 2026 | 5:00 PM | Deane L. Sadler Administration Building, 3205 West Davis, Conroe, TX 77304**
*Meeting may also be accessed virtually at: http://tiny.conroeisd.net/R78KV*

---

## Procedural Items
The following items are standard meeting procedures and will not be summarized individually:
- **I.A.** Invocation
- **I.B.** Pledge of Allegiance
- **V.** Citizen Participation
- **XII.** Take Requests from Trustees Regarding Future Board Agenda Items
- **XIII.** Adjourn

---

## Closed Session (Section II)

### II.A.1. Discuss Principal Candidate
**Context:** The Board will meet privately to discuss a candidate for a principal position. Under Texas law, personnel matters such as the appointment or employment of a public officer or employee may be discussed in a closed session, away from the public. No final action may be taken in closed session.

**Historical Summary:** No prior history available yet on this specific item.

---

### II.B.1. Update Pursuant to School Board Policy EHBAG (Local)
**Context:** The Board will receive a confidential legal update related to Policy EHBAG, which governs how CISD tracks and reports legal costs tied to special education. This closed session item likely involves legal counsel providing the Board with a confidential monthly report on special education-related legal fees, as required by the policy. Consultation with attorneys on matters involving pending or contemplated litigation may occur in closed session under Texas law.

**Historical Summary:** Policy EHBAG was adopted by the Board on September 16, 2025. That update established a brand-new Board Policy EHBAG (Local) on Special Education Legal Cost Transparency, creating requirements for a monthly confidential legal report to the Board and a quarterly public financial report on the district's website covering special education-related legal fees, with full implementation required no later than February 1, 2026. [View Policy EHBAG in Policy Tracker](https://cisd-policy.boardmonitor.app/policy.html?code=EHBAG)

---

### II.C.1. Regarding Potential Sale of Property
**Context:** The Board will meet privately to deliberate the potential sale of district-owned property. Texas law permits closed-session discussions on real estate transactions when conducting them in public could harm the district's negotiating position. Any final decision must be made in open session.

**Historical Summary:** No prior history available yet on this specific item.

---

## Action on Closed Session Items (Section III)

### III.A. Discussion and Possible Action Regarding Potential Sale of Property
**Context:** Following the closed-session discussion on a potential property sale, the Board will reconvene in open session to take any necessary formal vote or action. This is required by the Texas Open Meetings Act, which mandates that all final decisions be made publicly.

**Historical Summary:** No prior history available yet on this specific item.

---

**Context:** The Board will formally recognize Conroe High School's powerlifting team for winning the 2026 UIL/THSPA Class 6A State Championship. This is a ceremonial honor celebrating student athletic achievement at the state level.

**Context:** The Board will receive a presentation on the requirements of Texas Senate Bill 546, which relates to seat belts on school buses. This is an informational item likely addressing what the law requires of districts and what steps CISD may need to take to comply.

### IX.A. Consider and Possibly Approve Library Materials in Accordance with Texas Education Code §33.026 and Board Policy EFB (Local)
**Context:** The Board will consider approving a set of library materials for district schools, as required under Policy EFB and Texas Education Code §33.026. **Context:** The Board will vote to select a Construction Manager-at-Risk (CMR) — a contractor who manages the construction process and assumes financial risk for cost overruns — for renovation projects at The Woodlands 9th Grade campus and College Park High School. **Context:** The Board will vote to select a Construction Manager-at-Risk for renovations to an auxiliary district facility and authorize the Superintendent or designee to finalize the related contract. This item involves a significant capital expenditure.

### XI.E. Consider and Approve Annexation of San Jacinto Elementary Site into MUD 111 and Authorize the Superintendent to Negotiate and Execute Necessary Documents
**Context:** The Board will consider approving the annexation of the San Jacinto Elementary School property into Municipal Utility District (MUD) 111, which would bring the site under that utility district's service area for water, sewer, or other utility services. meeting_notes_md:
# Conroe ISD Board of Trustees – Unofficial Meeting Notes
**Date:** April 21, 2026
**Type:** Regular Meeting
**Time:** Called to order 5:04 PM; Adjourned 10:48 PM

## Meeting Highlights
*Summary generated 2026-04-23 00:21 UTC*

- The board approved the Conroe Virtual School expansion plan for 2026–27, adopting the Subject platform at $300/student (~$90,000 for ~300 students), approved 6–1, with the dissenting trustee citing concerns about combining the platform purchase and full plan into a single vote.
- The board adopted the Special Education Homecoming Plan to return students in specialized programs to their home zoned schools, approved 7–0; the plan includes $3.2 million in new special education staffing (15 dyslexia teachers, 4 speech pathologists, 4 diagnosticians, 4 psychologists) and is expected to save ~$800,000 by eliminating 8 bus routes.
- The board approved library materials for March and April, including opening-day collections for new campuses Fowler and Arnold, with CFO Dr. Vinson certifying compliance with TEC §33.026, board policy EFB, and Senate Bill 13, approved 7–0.
- The board approved the Cartwright Elementary mascot change from Cougar to Colts, following a request from the Cartwright family citing the family's deep ties to horsemanship, approved 7–0.
- The board approved the consent agenda 7–0, which included a budget amendment, employment of professional personnel (including new alternative programs principal Marcus Cowart), and the TCEQ-required Water Conservation and Drought Contingency Plan for the Moorhead/Caney Creek Water Plant Facility.
- The board approved annexation of San Jacinto Elementary to MUD 111, authorizing the superintendent to execute documents, which is expected to avoid ~$1.2 million in infrastructure costs and recover land for a parking lot and possible gymnasium, approved 7–0.
- The board tabled four planning and construction items 7–0 to allow further research, with items to return at the May 12th meeting.
- The board received an informational budget update projecting a balanced 2026–27 budget; a compensation plan vote — choosing between a $695,000 minimum-compliance model and a $2.563 million step-increase model — is scheduled for the June regular board meeting.

## Call to Order & Preliminaries

The regular meeting was called to order at 5:04 PM by Board President Odenweller. A quorum was present. Following the invocation by Trustee Gambino and the Pledge of Allegiance led by Scouts from Trinity Episcopal Church in the Woodlands (Troops 777/1777, Sea Scout Ship 777, Cub Scout Pack 777, and Venturing Crew 112), the board recessed into closed session at approximately 5:08 PM.

**Closed Session** (Texas Government Code §§551.074, 551.071, 551.072): Discussion of a principal candidate, a legal update, and potential sale of a property. The board returned to open session at 6:15 PM. No action was taken based on the closed session.

## Special Recognitions

### Dedication to Miss Ruby Monty
The board dedicated the evening's meeting to Miss Ruby Monty, a 35-year veteran educator and mother of Trustee May, who passed away the previous day. All educators in the room were asked to stand in her honor.

### Powerlifting State Champions
Dr. Murrell recognized three individual powerlifting state champions:

- **Aaron Quail** (Conroe High School, 242 lb unequipped division) — first-ever state champion in the unequipped category; set state records including a 375 lb bench press, 550 lb squat, and 555 lb deadlift (1,480 lb total)
- **Skyler McEldowncy** (Grand Oaks High School, 220 lb equipped division) — 485 lb bench press, 610 lb deadlift, 705 lb squat (1,800 lb total)
- **Eduardo Barrera** (The Woodlands High School, 114 lb division) — 255 lb bench, 470 lb squat, 445 lb deadlift (1,170 lb total); also awarded Best Platform Squat for the region; The Woodlands' ninth state champion

Coaches, principals, athletic coordinators, and families were also recognized.

### College Park Boys Soccer — First Team State Championship
Dr. Murrell recognized the College Park Cavaliers boys soccer team for winning the school's first-ever team athletic state championship. Season record: 19-3 overall, 12-2 in district, with 84 goals scored and 22 allowed. The team survived two double-overtime playoff games decided by penalty kicks. Individual honors:
- **Salem Sheriff** (#11) — 22 goals, 12 assists; State Tournament MVP
- **Alex Harkort** (#7) and **Sebastian Hernandez** (#1) — additional state tournament recognition
- **Miguel Basera** (#9) — goalkeeper recognition

Head coach Michael Jcowick and the College Park family, led by Principal Robert McFarland, were also recognized.

## Citizen Participation

Public comment was conducted in accordance with board policy BED ([Policy Tracker](https://cisd-policy.boardmonitor.app/policy.html?code=BED)). Each speaker received two minutes. The board listened but did not engage in discussion during this portion.

**Speakers and topics:**

- **Mason Neans** (5th grader, Clark Intermediate) — Requested more recess time and fewer computers in the classroom, citing concerns about screen distraction, particularly for students with ADHD.

- **TJ Herbert** (4th grader) — Thanked STEM teacher Miss Gaskins and asked the board to consider expanding the STEM program to all elementary campuses in the future, including his future school, Valor.

- **Ola Atony** (junior, Oakridge High School) — Proposed creation of a Superintendent's Student Council with representatives from each high school and a student trustee position who could attend board meetings and contribute to the public record, at no cost to the district.

- **Cadence "Sadi Beth" Walker** (senior, Caney Creek High School) — Spoke against the district's decision not to renew the Communities in Schools (CIS) contract, citing the program's critical role in meeting basic needs for students at Caney Creek (80% economically disadvantaged). Reported that she collected 1,038 signatures from staff, students, and families in three business days.

- **Bridget Neans** (parent) — Addressed concerns about excessive computer use in classrooms, particularly for students with ADHD and working memory challenges. Advocated for printed math tests as the default rather than an accommodation.

- **Katie Fischer** (parent volunteer, Wilkinson Elementary) — Spoke in support of the STEM program, praising teacher Miss Gaskins and describing the program as a place where every child can succeed.

- **Laura Mott** (parent, Wilkinson Elementary) — Requested the board bring back the STEM program when funding allows, and expand it to all elementary campuses.

- **Destinee Milton** — Criticized the reassignment of instructional behavioral coaches and removal of CIS, calling the board's public celebration of the "balanced budget" insensitive to affected employees. Stated that local businesses had offered to sponsor CIS and that a teacher who offered her own money to support her school's program faced an internal investigation. Asserted that over 26,000 students rely on CIS.

- **Yoshiko Gaio** (parent, The Woodlands High School) — Expressed concern about the district's decision to cancel the Japanese language program at TWHS. Noted that following parent advocacy, Japanese 2, 3, and 4 will continue via College Park's teacher, but Japanese 1 is not being offered to incoming freshmen, effectively phasing out a 36-year-old program. Proposed recruiting more students and applying for Japanese government-affiliated funding.

- **Courtney Berquette** (parent, The Woodlands High School) — Spoke in support of the Japanese exchange program, describing how it had personally impacted her family and her son's future plans to study in Japan.

- **Megan Gaskins** (18-year CISD educator, elementary STEM teacher) — Addressed the board in her own behalf, acknowledging difficult budget decisions but asking the board to bring back the STEM program when possible and to expand it to all elementary and intermediate campuses.

- **Susan Colleen Duhan** (32-year paraprofessional, Oakridge feeder zone) — Expressed concern about the lack of consistent discipline in schools and called on administrators to support teachers more firmly when students are disruptive.

- **Katie Moss** (PTO president, Wilkinson Intermediate) — Opposed the elimination of CIS and the behavior support program, describing both as lifelines for students at her Title I campus.

- **Masumi Reed** (retired Japanese teacher, TWHS, 1993–2017) — Expressed hope for continuation of the Japanese program and offered suggestions including seeking donations from Japanese companies and foundations.

- **Amanda Velator Toro** (parent and former educator) — Raised concerns about instructional delivery practices, including sit-and-get models, insufficient student breaks, and lack of project-based learning. Asked to know who she could follow up with.

- **Rebecca Tarvin** (staff, Wilkinson Elementary) — Spoke in support of the STEM program and asked the board to restore it when possible.

Cowart currently serves as Associate Principal of Operations at Conroe High School and has also served as Assistant Director of Transportation and Associate Principal of Special Populations. ## Cartwright Elementary Mascot Reconsideration

Dr. Klapesky presented a request from the Cartwright family to reconsider the school mascot. Background: during the original mascot vote, nominations included three horses and one cougar; the cougar was selected but with limited participation. The Cartwright family — for whom the school is named after Lynn Cartwright, a longtime Conroe High School employee who passed away in 2014 — has deep ties to horses and horsemanship through 4-H, FFA, and rodeo activities. They requested the mascot be changed to the **Colts**.

President Odenweller moved to rename the Cartwright mascot to the Colts.

**Vote: 7–0 (approved)**

## Senate Bill 546 — School Bus Seat Belt Report

Mr. McCord presented the required report under Senate Bill 546, which mandates that all school buses have three-point seat belts by 2029. Districts unable to meet that deadline financially must report to TEA by May. **No board vote was required.**

**Current fleet status (599 total buses):**
- 279 buses with three-point seat belts
- 246 buses with no seat belts
- 74 buses with two-point (lap) seat belts

**Cost estimates:**
- Retrofit all buses needing three-point belts: ~$10 million
- Purchase all new buses with three-point belts: ~$54 million
- Hybrid option (retrofit 2013+ buses, replace 2012 and older): a middle-cost alternative

Dr. Vinson stated it is not financially prudent to move forward with any of these solutions at this time. He noted that $12 million from Bond 2023 was used to purchase buses, which through grant funding was stretched to cover 19 buses instead of the originally planned 9. The transportation department was publicly recognized, with Juan Melendez and his team acknowledged for significant improvements in fleet maintenance, morale, and operations.

The board was informed of a bus driver recruiting fair on **April 29th at the Jet Center**.

Key discussion points:
- Supply chain constraints make bulk bus purchases nearly impossible even with funding
- Seat belt retrofit parts are also in short supply
- The mandate has no dedicated state funding attached to it
- The district spends approximately $35 million annually on transportation and receives approximately $7 million from the state

## Consider and Approve — Conroe Virtual School Plan

Dr. Murrell presented a proposal to revamp and expand the Conroe Virtual School for 2026–27 using a new learning platform called **Subject**.

**Current state:** Approximately 250 students in a hybrid synchronous/asynchronous model (grades 5–9 currently, expanding to 5–12). Teachers create all lessons, manage accommodations, mentor students, and communicate with families — described as an unsustainable workload.

**Proposed changes for 2026–27:**
- Move to a primarily **asynchronous** model using the Subject platform, which provides curriculum for grades 6–12
- CISD teachers remain the teachers of record, refocusing their time on mentoring, monitoring, parent communication, tutoring, and support rather than curriculum creation
- AI-powered tutoring adjusts to individual student needs
- Automated accommodation features for special education and 504 students
- Contract is annual (can exit after one year if needed)
- Cost: $300/student; estimated $90,000 for ~300 students, plus a district-wide license for remedial/credit recovery at no additional cost
- Subject also provides branding and staff development support
- Fifth grade enrollment is currently only ~10 students; sixth grade is ~19–20; the board discussed whether to serve grades 5–12 or scale back to 6–12 (Subject's curriculum does not include 5th grade)
- Enrollment goal: grow from ~250 to ~500 students in 2026–27, potentially to 1,000+ in 2027–28
- Open enrollment (accepting students from other districts) would be considered in 2027–28 pending board approval

**Key discussion points:**
- 244 CISD students currently enrolled in Hallsville ISD's Texas Virtual School Network, which is closing after next year; the district hopes to recapture those students
- ~100 current virtual students are special education or 504 students; anxiety, medical conditions, and scheduling conflicts are common reasons for enrollment
- Trustees expressed support for the virtual school as essential for the future while also wanting to investigate why students are leaving brick-and-mortar campuses
- Trustee May asked about curriculum vetting; Dr. Murrell confirmed Subject was asked specifically about content compliance with district policies and state law, and that CISD teachers will monitor the curriculum; all district contracts require vendors to comply with board policy and state law
- Trustee Duncan raised concerns about attendance accountability; Dr. Lucas noted that Senate Bill 569 will bring clearer attendance rules, and that the school has conducted approximately 1,000 parent-teacher conferences for 250 students this year to address engagement
- Long-term plan: virtual school will move to a dedicated space at the new Washington High School campus

**Vote: 6–1 (approved)**
*(The dissenting trustee stated they were "completely okay with Subject" but felt it was a big jump to combine the platform purchase and the full virtual school plan into one vote.)*

## District Staffing Report

Dr. Vinson and Dr. Landry presented a comprehensive overview of district staffing restructuring. **This was an informational presentation; no vote was taken.**

**Key themes:**
- Central office is being reorganized to reduce redundancy and improve support to campus principals
- Previously, multiple departments gave campuses different guidance; the new model consolidates curriculum, instruction, and school improvement under one aligned structure
- Curriculum coordinators will own specific subject areas and grade bands (elementary, middle, high) to ensure vertical alignment within feeder patterns
- The counseling and behavioral services structure has been reorganized under Dr. Taylor and Dr. Upshaw; 183 counselors across the district will now have clearer, standardized support and delivery systems
- A new MTSS director will design and implement consistent behavior management and classroom management plans across campuses
- **Financial savings:** The central office restructuring resulted in a net reduction of 28.5 FTEs and approximately $1.5–1.7 million in savings
- **No employees are losing their jobs** — all affected staff have been placed in campus positions

Dr. Vinson emphasized that the goal was to impact campuses and classrooms as little as possible and that any sacrifice should start at the central office level. He framed the reorganization as shifting from a scattershot advisory model to a focused, ownership-based instructional support model.

## Library Materials Approval

The board considered and voted on approval of library materials for March and April in compliance with Texas Education Code §33.026, board policy EFB ([Policy Tracker](https://cisd-policy.boardmonitor.app/policy.html?code=EFB)), and Senate Bill 13. The lists included books for existing campuses as well as opening-day collections for two new schools: **Fowler** and **Arnold**.

Staff noted the district has implemented **Bookmark**, a Texas-specific analytical tool to assist librarians and staff in reviewing library materials for compliance with SB 11/SB 13 and board policy. The tool flags books for human review based on professional reviews, challenge history in other districts, and content concerns — it does not replace human decision-making. Trustees emphasized that the tool assists but does not replace librarians and staff who make final determinations.

Dr. Vinson was asked to certify that the library materials presented comply with TEC §33.026, board policy EFB, and Senate Bill 13. He certified that they do.

**Vote: 7–0 (approved)**

## Special Education Homecoming Project and Plan

Dr. Landry presented the district's Special Education Homecoming Plan, a multi-year structural initiative to return students in specialized programs to their home zoned schools rather than busing them to centralized program locations. **The board voted to adopt the plan.**

**Key details:**
- This is year 3–4 of a phased rollout; secondary schools have already transitioned; this is the final phase for remaining programs
- **Not a budget-driven decision** — has been in development for years
- Students benefit from staying with neighborhood peers and siblings; families benefit from proximity; teachers benefit from greater flexibility
- **Estimated savings:** ~$800,000 from eliminating 8 bus routes; also frees up 8 drivers for unfilled routes
- All campuses receiving programs were built to accommodate special education; no new construction is needed
- Training timeline: Principal training begins May 4th; parent/student campus orientations by end of first week of May; ongoing training with Region 4 and in-house staff through summer
- **No students will be cut from services;** the district is also investing an additional $3.2 million in special education staff:
  - 15 new dyslexia teachers
  - 4 new speech pathologists
  - 4 new diagnosticians
  - 4 new school psychologists
- Trustee May raised concerns about the pace of change and praised Dr. Winkler and Dr. Wiggins for their responsiveness. Multiple trustees noted their concerns had been addressed in pre-meeting conversations.
- Trustee May also asked about special education staff retention; Dr. Vinson noted that manageability of workload and adequate support are key factors, and that clearer behavior management expectations will help
- One-to-one student support paras are assigned to specific students and travel with them; they are not counted in campus allocation ratios

**Vote: 7–0 (approved)**

## Texas Standard — District-Wide Character and Wellness Initiative

Dr. Taylor, Dr. Gorman, and members of the Texas Standard Task Force (including campus principals Dr. Colton Todd, Amber De Bulmont, Laura Collins, and Josh Hughes) presented information on the **Texas Standard**, a district-wide framework. **This was an informational presentation; no vote was taken.**

**Purpose:** A unified, district-wide movement to build student character, relationships, career readiness, and behavioral expectations — connecting to board goals around hope, well-being, engagement, and achievement.

**Four core strands:**
1. **Cultivate Meaningful Relationships** — Every student connected to a trusted adult
2. **Recognize Strengths and Interests** — Personal interest exploration and strength identification
3. **Your Future, Your Path** — Career exploration ("Future Fridays," industry videos, blue-collar through professional careers)
4. **Character Education and Behavior Standards** — District character traits (integrity, responsibility, gratitude, resilience); district-wide classroom behavior expectations based on PBIS (Positive Behavioral Interventions and Supports)

**Implementation details:**
- A 35-member task force including principals, APs, counselors, and teachers has been developing the framework
- This is described as a rebranding and systemizing of work already underway at many campuses, not a brand-new initiative
- Each campus will retain its own school identity/mascot and behavioral acronym (e.g., Wilkinson's ROAR, Grand Oaks' GROWL), with the Texas Standard as the district-wide overlay
- Teacher training guides will be delivered by campus principals
- A designated Texas Standard calendar will coordinate recognition events across the district
- Standard classroom management best practices will be implemented and monitored across all classrooms

**Trustee concerns and discussion:**
- Trustee Horton asked how results will be measured; Dr. Taylor confirmed that each feeder will have an assigned person monitoring discipline referrals and student behavioral data, with periodic reports to the board
- Trustee Duncan emphasized the need for swift, consistent disciplinary consequences alongside positive reinforcement — not just PBIS rewards — to improve teacher retention and campus safety
- Dr. Vinson noted HB 6 changes that now allow out-of-school suspension for K–2 students and other stronger consequence tools
- Dr. Taylor stated that campus principals behind her "are not giving suckers to kids that misbehave" and that the framework maintains accountability alongside relationship-building

## Budget Update — Preliminary 2026–27 Information

Chief Financial Officer Karen Garza and compensation consultant Amy Campbell (TASBY) presented a preliminary budget update. **This was an informational presentation; no vote was taken.**

**Background — The $8 million projected deficit:**
- In January/February, the district identified a projected $8 million gap: ~$2 million net revenue increase vs. ~$10 million in additional projected expenditures
- Since then, the district has completed a staffing study and made adjustments; the board was told a **balanced budget is anticipated for 2026–27**

**Key budget assumptions for 2026–27:**
- Budgeted enrollment: 72,800 (down from 73,500 this year; decline of ~700 students)
- Property value growth assumed at 5% → projected M&O tax rate compression to approximately $0.9348
- Tax rate not final until certified values received in July
- District retains commitment to 28-cent debt service tax rate (as promised to voters in Bond 2023)

**Notable financial highlights cited:**
- $17 million hold-harmless calculation error by the state (discovered in late 2024) helped bridge prior gaps
- Over $7 million in grant funding secured to expand bus purchases beyond bond authorization (12 buses → 19)
- ~$1 million in FEMA reimbursements recovered from Hurricane Barrel damage (75% of incurred damages)

**Revenue projections:**
- Tax revenue increase: ~$2.2 million
- State funding relatively flat; $2.3 million placeholder for special education intensity-based formula funding increase (conservative estimate)
- Pass-through items for teacher incentive allotment and TRS on-behalf

**Proposed personnel changes (net):**
- Central office restructuring: -28.5 FTEs, savings of $1.536 million
- Campus staffing: -51 FTEs, savings of $4.7 million (driven by enrollment decline, removal of 7–12 instructional coach model, partially offset by new campus openings)
- Special education: +34.5 FTEs, cost of $3.2 million
- Operations/support (primarily custodians and police for 3 new campuses): +38 FTEs, cost of $2.25 million
- **Net total: +4 FTEs, net cost of $88,000**
- 11 contingency units held in reserve to respond to enrollment surprises in August
- **No employees are losing their jobs** — all affected staff placed in campus positions

**Compensation plan:**
- Amy Campbell (TASBY) presented a market comparison showing CISD teacher salaries approximately $2,000 below market median
- Two models proposed:
  - **Model 1:** $695,000 total — covers only the statutorily required raises for teachers hitting the 3- and 5-year marks under House Bill 2; $673,500 of this is pass-through funding
  - **Model 2:** $2.563 million total — adds step increases for all teachers (locally funded)
- Model 2 is currently built into the preliminary budget; the board will choose between models at the June meeting
- **Compensation plan vote is scheduled for the June regular board meeting**

**Communities in Schools (CIS):**
- The $42,000 placeholder for CIS remains in the expenditure budget
- Dr. Vinson acknowledged the public concern raised during citizen participation and stated he and Dr. Taylor are working on a plan to fill gaps left by the removal of CIS, particularly at high-need campuses like Caney Creek; discussions are ongoing about how to preserve mentoring and other services

**Looking ahead to 2027–28:**
- Four large new campuses opening (including high schools and junior highs) will add significant costs for staffing, utilities, athletics, and UIL programs
- Fast Growth Allotment from the state is declining as enrollment growth flattens; potential loss of $5–9 million in funding
- 2027–28 is a legislative year — additional funding possible but uncertain

**Legislative priorities identified:**
- Special education funding (15% annual growth; district allocates significantly more than state allotment covers)
- Dyslexia funding gap
- Transportation funding ($35M district cost vs. $7M state reimbursement)
- Safety and security
- Staff estimated that fully funding these three buckets alone would provide approximately $58 million in additional annual revenue

**Fund balance:**
- Current unassigned fund balance projected at end of fiscal year: ~$50.8 million
- District target (20–25% of budget): ~$154 million
- Fund balance is below target primarily due to health fund transfers: ~$8 million planned transfer this year; ~$7 million last year ($15 million over two years)
- The district is currently running a ~$5 million deficit through end of March

## Consider and Approve — San Jacinto Elementary Annexation to MUD 111

Mr. McCord presented a request related to San Jacinto Elementary, originally built in 1980 in a rural area with no utilities, requiring the district to build its own water well and wastewater treatment plant. The adjacent Arvilla development now has utilities via MUD 111 that the district could connect to.

**Benefits of annexation:**
- Recover approximately half an acre on the northeastern portion of the property to build a needed parking lot and potentially a gymnasium
- Avoid approximately $1.2 million in upcoming lifecycle work on the existing well and wastewater treatment plant
- The board action authorizes Dr. Vinson to execute documents and submit petitions for annexation to MUD 111, the City of Conroe (ETJ release), the Texas Attorney General, and the MUD 111 board

**Note:** Board Policy CV ([Policy Tracker](https://cisd-policy.boardmonitor.app/policy.html?code=CV)) delegates authority to the superintendent to execute public utility agreements for campuses; this board action was required to initiate the annexation process.

**Vote: 7–0 (approved)**
